title: Biological robustness
main_claim: Biological systems can achieve robustness through architectural features such as buffering, modularity, feedback, and network organization.
limitations: Conceptual review; not specific to glycosylation

title: Pervasive robustness in biological systems
main_claim: Robustness is widespread and can be studied across molecular, developmental, and systems levels.
limitations: Conceptual review; requires translation into pathway-level tests

title: The causes of evolvability and their evolution
main_claim: Evolvability depends on mechanisms that generate heritable adaptive phenotypic variation, including robustness and landscape structure.
limitations: Not pathway-specific

title: Degeneracy and complexity in biological systems
journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
main_claim: Degeneracy can support both robustness and evolvability in complex biological systems.
limitations: Older conceptual paper; needs careful connection to data

title: Evolutionary forces shaping the Golgi glycosylation machinery: why cell surface glycans are universal to living cells
journal: Cold Spring Harbor Perspectives in Biology
main_claim: Golgi glycosylation generates cell-surface glycan diversity shaped by multiple evolutionary forces.
limitations: Review; broad across glycan classes

title: Glycointeractions in bacterial pathogenesis
main_claim: Glycan-mediated host-pathogen interactions are central to bacterial colonization, persistence, and disease.
limitations: Mostly bacterial pathogenesis; not N-glycosylation-specific

title: Protein-glycan interactions in the control of innate and adaptive immune responses
main_claim: Protein-glycan interactions regulate pathogen recognition, immune modulation, homeostasis, and inflammation.
limitations: Review; broad glycan focus

title: The Glycoscience of Immunity
main_claim: Glycan composition shapes immune protein interactions and immune system function.
limitations: Review; needs gene-level extraction

title: N-Glycosylation and Inflammation; the Not-So-Sweet Relation
main_claim: N-glycosylation changes contribute to inflammatory disease processes and immune-cell function.
limitations: Review; disease links may be context-dependent

title: Congenital Disorders of N-Linked Glycosylation and Multiple Pathway Overview
main_claim: N-linked CDGs affect core and overlapping glycosylation machinery and provide a disease framework.
limitations: GeneReviews is curated but not designed for enrichment testing

title: Centralized Modularity of N-Linked Glycosylation Pathways in Mammalian Cells
main_claim: N-linked glycosylation pathways can be represented as modular networks branching from a common upstream region.
limitations: Topology depends on pathway encoding and curated reaction set; use as conceptual and methodological support, not final evidence.

title: Distribution of events of positive selection and population differentiation in a metabolic pathway: the case of asparagine N-glycosylation
authors: Dall'Olio; Laayouni; Luisi; Sikora; Montanucci; et al.
main_claim: Original pathway-level scan reported stronger population differentiation and selection-related signals in parts of the N-glycosylation pathway.
limitations: Uses older HGDP/SNP-array style resources and methods now requiring stronger demographic, LD, and regional controls.

title: N-Glycans
main_claim: Authoritative pathway overview for N-glycan biosynthesis, processing, structures, and enzymes.
limitations: Reference chapter, not an evolutionary analysis; use for pathway curation and terminology.

title: Roles of N-linked glycans in the endoplasmic reticulum
main_claim: N-linked glycans are central to ER folding, quality control, and glycoprotein maturation.
limitations: Focuses on ER biology rather than evolutionary/disease gradients.

title: N-linked protein glycosylation in the ER
main_claim: Reviews the conserved ER machinery for lipid-linked oligosaccharide synthesis and transfer to proteins.
important_genes_or_pathways: LLO synthesis; OST complex; ER processing
limitations: Mechanistic review; evolutionary framing must be added by this project.

title: Mammalian glycosylation in immunity
main_claim: Mammalian glycans and their biosynthetic enzymes regulate immune recognition, signaling complexes, and immune-cell function.
limitations: Broad glycan focus; gene-level relevance needs extraction.

title: Glycans in virus-host interactions: a structural perspective
main_claim: Host and viral glycans act in attachment, immune evasion, antigenicity, and host range.
limitations: Mostly structural/virology; not pathway-region specific.

title: Adaptive immune activation: glycosylation does matter
main_claim: Glycosylation affects antigen uptake, processing, presentation, and adaptive immune activation.
limitations: Review-level support; needs careful separation of N-linked and other glycans.

title: Network inference from glycoproteomics data reveals new reactions in the IgG glycosylation pathway
authors: Benedetti; Pucic-Bakovic; Keser; Wahl; Hassinen; et al.
main_claim: Glycoproteomic data can infer reaction relationships and regulatory structure in IgG N-glycosylation.
limitations: Specific to IgG and plasma glycans; pathway inference assumptions need review.

title: Multivariate discovery and replication of five novel loci associated with Immunoglobulin G N-glycosylation
main_claim: Multivariate GWAS of IgG N-glycosylation identifies loci affecting IgG glycan traits and expands genetic evidence for immune glycan regulation.
limitations: Phenotype is IgG-specific; gene assignment and functional interpretation require follow-up.

title: Glycosylation of immunoglobulin G is regulated by a large network of genes pleiotropic with inflammatory diseases
authors: Klaric; Tsepilov; Stanton; Mangino; Sikka; et al.
main_claim: Genetic loci associated with IgG glycosylation form a regulatory network and overlap inflammatory disease biology.
limitations: Phenotype is IgG-specific and downstream from many systemic effects; not direct evidence for all N-glycosylation genes.

title: Genome-wide association study on immunoglobulin G glycosylation patterns
authors: Wahl; van den Akker; Klaric; Spector; et al.
main_claim: GWAS identifies loci influencing IgG glycan traits and links glycosylation variation to immune traits.
limitations: Complex trait associations are indirect and context dependent.

title: Recent and ongoing selection in the human genome
main_claim: Reviews genomic signatures of recent selection and their interpretation limits in humans.
limitations: Pre-rare-variant era review; still useful for conceptual framing.

title: A map of recent positive selection in the human genome
main_claim: Introduced/applied iHS genome-wide to identify recent incomplete sweep candidates in HapMap populations.
limitations: Selection signals are sensitive to phasing, demography, SNP ascertainment, and local recombination.

title: Genome-wide detection and characterization of positive selection in human populations
authors: Sabeti; Varilly; Fry; Lohmueller; Hostetter; et al.
main_claim: Applied haplotype-based scans for recent positive selection across human populations.
limitations: Outlier scans require caution and functional validation.

title: The role of geography in human adaptation
authors: Coop; Pickrell; Novembre; Kudaravalli; Li; et al.
main_claim: Geographic allele-frequency patterns can identify adaptation candidates but require population-structure-aware interpretation.
limitations: Geographic correlation does not by itself establish adaptation or causal gene assignment.

title: A population genetic signal of polygenic adaptation
main_claim: Introduces a framework for detecting coordinated shifts in trait-associated alleles across populations.
limitations: Later work exposed sensitivity of polygenic adaptation claims to GWAS bias and population structure.

title: Reduced signal for polygenic adaptation of height in UK Biobank
authors: Berg; Harpak; Sinnott-Armstrong; Joergensen; Mostafavi; et al.
main_claim: Demonstrates how uncorrected stratification can create apparent polygenic adaptation signals.
limitations: Height-specific case study, but highly relevant to cautious interpretation.

title: High-coverage whole-genome sequencing of the expanded 1000 Genomes Project cohort including 602 trios
authors: Byrska-Bishop; Evani; Zhao; Basile; Abel; et al.
main_claim: Provides high-coverage open human variation resource suitable for modern population-genetic validation.
limitations: Not deeply phenotyped; population labels still require careful interpretation.

title: Network biology: understanding the cell's functional organization
main_claim: Cellular networks show modular, hierarchical, and hub-like organization relevant to robustness and disease.
limitations: General network review; glycosylation graph must be defined independently.

title: Lethality and centrality in protein networks
main_claim: Protein network connectivity correlates with essentiality in yeast, motivating centrality as a candidate constraint feature.
limitations: Classic but interaction-network biases and centrality definitions require caution.

title: Influence of metabolic network structure and function on enzyme evolution
main_claim: Links metabolic network topology and flux-related features to enzyme evolutionary rates and duplication.
limitations: Not glycosylation-specific; flux data may not transfer directly to glycan processing.

title: Comparative genomics of centrality and essentiality in three eukaryotic protein-interaction networks
main_claim: Centrality-essentiality relationships vary across networks and organisms, warning against overgeneralized hub claims.
limitations: Network ascertainment and definition strongly affect conclusions.

title: The human disease network
authors: Goh; Cusick; Valle; Childs; Vidal; Barabasi
journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
main_claim: Human disease genes form a network linking disorders and molecular modules.
limitations: Useful for disease-network framing, not specific to glycosylation.

title: The mutational constraint spectrum quantified from variation in 141,456 humans
authors: Karczewski; Francioli; Tiao; Cummings; Alfoldi; et al.
main_claim: gnomAD quantifies gene-level intolerance to loss-of-function and missense variation, including LOEUF.
limitations: Constraint depends on annotation, power, expression, and disease ascertainment; use with covariates.

title: Analysis of protein-coding genetic variation in 60,706 humans
authors: Lek; Karczewski; Minikel; Samocha; Banks; et al.
main_claim: ExAC established large-scale human coding variation and gene constraint metrics for rare disease interpretation.
limitations: Superseded in part by gnomAD, but foundational.

title: A systematic survey of loss-of-function variants in human protein-coding genes
authors: MacArthur; Balasubramanian; Frankish; Huang; Morris; et al.
main_claim: Humans carry many predicted loss-of-function variants, requiring stringent annotation and interpretation.
limitations: Variant annotation challenges are central; use for cautionary framing.

title: ClinVar: public archive of interpretations of clinically relevant variants
authors: Landrum; Lee; Benson; Brown; Chao; et al.
main_claim: ClinVar aggregates clinical interpretations of human variants and their disease relationships.
limitations: Submitter heterogeneity and conflicting assertions require filtering.

title: The NHGRI-EBI GWAS Catalog of published genome-wide association studies, targeted arrays and summary statistics
authors: Buniello; MacArthur; Cerezo; Harris; Hayhurst; et al.
main_claim: GWAS Catalog provides curated associations for complex traits and diseases.
limitations: Gene assignment and trait ontology need careful handling.

title: Treatment Options in Congenital Disorders of Glycosylation
main_claim: Reviews CDG disease mechanisms and available or emerging treatments.
limitations: Treatment-focused rather than architecture-focused.

title: Congenital Disorders of Glycosylation: What Clinicians Need to Know?
main_claim: Clinical review of CDG diagnosis, presentation, and classification.
limitations: Clinical framing; gene table extraction needed before quantitative use.

title: Sloppiness, robustness, and evolvability in systems biology
main_claim: Biochemical network models often have many sloppy parameter directions and few stiff combinations, providing a nonadaptive route to robustness and evolvability.
limitations: Conceptual/modeling review; does not directly test pathway region gradients.

title: Bow ties, metabolism and disease
main_claim: Bow-tie architectures mediate tradeoffs among efficiency, robustness, evolvability, and characteristic fragilities in biological and technological networks.
limitations: Architecture analogy is useful but must be operationalized for N-glycosylation rather than asserted.

title: Evolution of Bow-Tie Architectures in Biology
main_claim: Evolutionary simulations show bow-tie structures can emerge when input-output goals are compressible and mutational processes reduce unnecessary links.
limitations: Theoretical model; useful for framing but not evidence that N-glycosylation evolved by this mechanism.

title: Biological roles of glycans
main_claim: Complex glycans have diverse biological roles across evolution, including microbial binding, recognition, and rapidly evolving cell-surface functions.
limitations: Broad glycan review; not N-glycosylation-region specific.

title: Colloquium paper: uniquely human evolution of sialic acid genetics and biology
journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
main_claim: Human sialic-acid biology includes multiple uniquely human changes affecting cell-surface glycans, Siglec biology, tissue specificity, and pathogen regimes.
limitations: Sialic-acid focused and partly human-specific; should support interface evolution, not the whole N-glycosylation pathway.

title: Multifarious roles of sialic acids in immunity
journal: Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences
main_claim: Reviews sialic-acid roles in immunity, including Siglecs, selectins, complement regulation, microbial mimicry, pathogen binding, and immune modulation.
limitations: Review-level evidence; not restricted to N-linked glycans.

title: Multiple Genomic Events Altering Hominin SIGLEC Biology and Innate Immunity Predated the Common Ancestor of Humans and Archaic Hominins
authors: Khan; de Manuel; Peyregne; Do; Prufer; Marques-Bonet; Varki; Gagneux; Varki
main_claim: Multiple SIGLEC changes affecting sialic-acid self-recognition and innate immunity occurred before the common ancestor of modern and archaic hominins.
limitations: Focuses on lectin receptors rather than glycosylation enzymes; useful for interface coevolution framing.

title: Glycan diversity in the course of vertebrate evolution
main_claim: Vertebrate evolution retained numerous and sometimes redundant glycosyltransferase genes and species-specific glycan structures.
limitations: Review; broad across glycan classes and vertebrates.

title: Investigation of glycan evolution based on a comprehensive analysis of glycosyltransferases using phylogenetic profiling
main_claim: Phylogenetic profiling of 173 human glycosyltransferases and linked enzymes suggests non-reducing terminal glycan synthesis is enriched for newly evolved glycosyltransferases.
limitations: Phylogenetic-profile approach depends on orthology calls and broad enzyme grouping.

title: Evolution of fucosyltransferase genes in vertebrates
authors: Costache; Apoil; Cailleau; Elmgren; Larson; Henry; Blancher; Iordachescu; Oriol; Mollicone
main_claim: Fucosyltransferase genes evolved through duplications, translocations, and divergence, with human FUT3/FUT5/FUT6 cluster duplications placed between mammalian radiation and human-chimpanzee divergence.
limitations: Older family-specific study; useful as an example rather than a general result.

title: Glycans as critical regulators of gut immunity in homeostasis and disease
authors: Dias; Pereira; Padrao; Alves; Marcos-Pinto; Lago; Pinho
main_claim: Glycans participate in regulatory circuits of innate and adaptive gut immunity, and glycan changes can push inflammatory or anti-inflammatory responses.
limitations: Review; includes multiple glycan classes and is not pathway-region specific.

title: How bacterial pathogens of the gastrointestinal tract use the mucosal glyco-code to harness mucus and microbiota
main_claim: Gastrointestinal pathogens exploit host mucus, mucosal glycans, and microbiota-associated nutrient and information flows during infection.
limitations: Mostly mucosal and pathogen biology; N-linked enzyme links require extraction.

title: Mucosal glycans: key drivers of the development of inflammatory bowel disease and a potential new therapeutic target
main_claim: Mucosal glycans contribute to epithelial barrier integrity and host-microbiota immune crosstalk, with implications for inflammatory bowel disease and therapy.
limitations: New 2026 review; use for current framing but separate mucosal/O-glycan evidence from N-glycosylation-specific claims.

title: Natural selection on the peptide-binding regions of major histocompatibility complex molecules
main_claim: MHC peptide-binding regions are a classic example of selection maintaining functionally relevant immune variation.
important_genes_or_pathways: MHC class I and class II peptide-binding regions
limitations: Older review; useful as comparator framing rather than direct method template.

title: Human MHC architecture and evolution: implications for disease association studies
main_claim: Human MHC architecture combines extreme variation, linkage disequilibrium, disease association, and evolutionary history.
limitations: MHC is far more polymorphic and structurally complex than N-glycosylation; use as an interface comparator, not a direct analogue.

title: The human olfactory receptor gene family
journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
main_claim: The human olfactory receptor family is large, clustered, evolutionarily dynamic, and rich in pseudogenes.
limitations: Comparator for environmental sensing, but receptor-family dynamics differ from enzyme pathway architecture.

title: Protein folding and quality control in the ER
journal: Cold Spring Harbor Perspectives in Biology
main_claim: ER protein folding and quality-control systems maintain proteostasis through folding assistance, surveillance, and degradation.
important_genes_or_pathways: ER protein folding; ER quality control; ERAD
limitations: Robustness-heavy comparator; not a diversity-generating interface system.

title: Molecular Evolution and Network-Level Analysis of the N-Glycosylation Metabolic Pathway Across Primates
main_claim: Primate N-glycosylation genes are strongly conserved with no clear protein-coding positive selection signal, and pathway/network connectivity is associated with purifying constraint.
important_genes_or_pathways: N-glycosylation pathway; degree centrality; pathway position
limitations: Directly relevant predecessor; implies downstream/interface evolvability should not be framed mainly as accelerated coding-sequence evolution.

title: Epigenetic regulation of protein glycosylation
main_claim: Glycan structures arise from a dynamic network of glyco-genes whose expression can be epigenetically regulated; core N-glycosylation is essential while antenna and terminal modifications contribute to individual variation and environmental response.
important_genes_or_pathways: MGAT4A; B4GALNT2; ABO transferases; FUT3; FUT7; sialyltransferases; sulfotransferases
limitations: Review-level and early-stage epigenetic evidence; many examples are cancer or non-N-glycan-specific, so use for framing and regulatory hypotheses.

title: Protein glycosylation - an evolutionary crossroad between genes and environment
main_claim: Protein glycosylation is proposed as an interface between genetic information and environmental responsiveness because glycan outputs depend on regulated glyco-gene networks rather than direct sequence templates.
limitations: Conceptual review; needs empirical follow-up with modern glycome genetics and pathway-region tests.

title: Complex genetic regulation of protein glycosylation
main_claim: Human glycome traits show complex genetic regulation, motivating glycan-output phenotypes as measurable downstream consequences of glyco-gene variation.
limitations: Early glycome genetics; sample size and assay scope are smaller than later GWAS resources.

title: Genomics Meets Glycomics - The First GWAS Study of Human N-Glycome Identifies HNF1A as a Master Regulator of Plasma Protein Fucosylation
authors: Huffman; Knezevic; Vitart; Kattla; Adamczyk; et al.
main_claim: First plasma N-glycome GWAS identified HNF1A and fucosylation-related loci, showing that glycan-output variation can be mapped genetically and often involves regulatory control.
limitations: Plasma N-glycome integrates many proteins and tissues; locus-to-gene assignment and pathway-region mapping need care.

title: Glycans - the third revolution in evolution
main_claim: Glycans are argued to add an evolvable biological information layer because glycan structures are generated by regulated networks and can vary without direct protein-sequence change.
limitations: Conceptual and broad; should support the model framing, not stand as direct evidence for N-glycosylation pathway-region gradients.

title: Loci associated with N-glycosylation of human immunoglobulin G show pleiotropy with autoimmune diseases and haematological cancers
authors: Lauc; Huffman; Pucic; Zgaga; et al.
main_claim: IgG N-glycosylation GWAS identified loci influencing antibody glycan traits and showed pleiotropy with autoimmune diseases and hematological cancers.
important_genes_or_pathways: IgG N-glycosylation loci; immune disease loci
limitations: IgG-specific phenotype; glycan variation may reflect immune-cell state, regulatory effects, or trans control rather than direct adaptation of pathway enzymes.

title: Defining the genetic control of human blood plasma N-glycome using genome-wide association study
main_claim: Expanded plasma N-glycome GWAS identified replicated loci and implicated both glycosylation enzymes and broader regulatory biology in plasma glycan-output variation.
important_genes_or_pathways: Plasma N-glycome loci; glycosyltransferases; regulatory loci
limitations: Plasma glycome is a composite phenotype; direct pathway-region interpretation requires mapping loci to genes and glycan structures.

title: Fine-Mapping of the Human Blood Plasma N-Glycome onto Its Proteome
main_claim: Protein-resolved mapping of plasma N-glycans helps connect bulk plasma glycome signals to specific glycoproteins and proteomic context.
limitations: Protein-specific resolution helps interpretation but is still plasma-biased and not a complete pathway-region test.

title: Multivariate Genome-Wide Association Study Identifies New Loci Associated With IgG N-Glycosylation
main_claim: Multivariate IgG N-glycosylation GWAS identified additional loci and reinforced that antibody glycan-output variation has a distributed genetic architecture.
limitations: IgG-specific and association-based; gene assignment and causal mechanisms require follow-up.

title: Genome-wide association study of the human plasma N-glycome identifies genetic links to liver diseases and anti-inflammatory proteins
main_claim: Large plasma N-glycome GWAS links glycan traits to many loci, liver-related biology, anti-inflammatory proteins, and disease-relevant pathways, strengthening glycan-output genetics as a modern evidence stream.
important_genes_or_pathways: Plasma N-glycome loci; liver disease; anti-inflammatory proteins
limitations: Very recent large-scale association study; pathway-region conclusions require careful locus-to-gene and phenotype-to-glycan interpretation.
